title: Chapter 10 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Rescue blocks, Ensures, and Raising Exceptions!

questions.question: What actually IS an Exception in Ruby?
options: A physical Object created from an Error class to officially represent a crash state.
questions.explanation: Because 'Everything is an Object' in Ruby, even the errors themselves are just shiny objects created from Error subclasses!

questions.question: If an Exception is raised and your code doesn't do anything to catch it, what does Ruby automatically do by default?
options: It returns `nil` and continues executing the next line.
It instantly terminates the absolute entire program right there.
questions.explanation: Unhandled exceptions are fatal. They instantly kill the entire script.

questions.question: Which keyword acts as the safety net, catching falling Exceptions and preventing the whole script from terminating?
questions.explanation: If the code above generates an Exception, it is passed down into the `rescue` block to be handled.

questions.question: What is the very first keyword you must type to start wrapping dangerous code before the net?
questions.explanation: A complete safety block is constructed using `begin...rescue...end`.

questions.question: What happens if a `ZeroDivisionError` occurs inside a block that explicitly strictly says `rescue TypeError`?
options: The error passes flawlessly straight through the net un-rescued and instantly crashes the program.
questions.explanation: By explicitly naming the exact error class, you tell Ruby 'ONLY rescue this specific type of object. Let everything else die'.

questions.question: How can you capture the actual Exception object itself inside the rescue block so you can deeply examine it using `puts`?
questions.explanation: The elegant rocket `=>` intelligently assigns the thrown Exception object to the massive temporary variable `e`.

questions.question: Which keyword acts as the absolute final safeguard, flawlessly guaranteeing its code will run even if a fatal unhandled Exception destroys the block above it?
questions.explanation: `ensure` is universally used specifically for critical cleanup operations like safely closing database connections.

questions.question: If your `begin` block runs perfectly with zero exceptions, the `ensure` block is skipped.
questions.explanation: `ensure` ALWAYS reliably runs. That's its entire purpose. It is the final guaranteed step of the block execution regardless of success or crash.

questions.question: Which explicit keyword deliberately throws an Exception object into the system to aggressively crash the program?
questions.explanation: You manually `raise` the alarm precisely when your logic catches something incredibly illegal happening.

questions.question: If you brutally type `raise "I am dying!"` without explicitly stating a Class Name, what type of Exception Class does Ruby automatically default to?
questions.explanation: Supplying just a naked string intelligently defaults to raising a standard `RuntimeError` exception object containing that string message.

questions.question: If you brilliantly want to construct your very own Custom Error class called `BankError`, what single massive parent class MUST you mathematically inherit from?
questions.explanation: Inheriting `class BankError < StandardError` grants your empty class all the violent crashing capabilities while perfectly keeping it safe to rescue.

questions.question: Why shouldn't custom errors violently inherit completely from the top-level `Exception` class?
options: A naked `rescue` block ONLY intelligently catches `StandardError` and its children. It completely misses raw `Exceptions`.
questions.explanation: If you inherit from `Exception`, standard empty `rescue` blocks will let your error fly straight through and destroy the program.

questions.question: If you try to divide any Integer by exactly zero, Ruby instantly aggressively throws a _____Error.
questions.explanation: The exact class name is `ZeroDivisionError`.

questions.question: If you try to execute a method that does not physically exist anywhere on the Object, what error does Ruby throw?
questions.explanation: Calling `"hello".bark` violently triggers a `NoMethodError` because strings can't bark!

questions.question: If an Exception is successfully rescued, does the program terminate immediately after the `rescue` block finishes?
options: Yes, the rescue block is the last thing it does.
No, the script delightfully survives and continues peacefully executing the code underneath the `end` keyword.
questions.explanation: Because the Exception net successfully caught the bomb, the rest of the script is completely safe to run.

questions.question: Look at: `rescue StandardError => e`. What exactly is the variable `e` now holding?
options: The entire, physical Exception Object that crashed the system.
questions.explanation: You explicitly saved the instantiated blast object directly into `e`.

questions.question: If you raise an exception, what instantly happens to the code physically below the `raise` keyword inside that exact method?
options: It is aggressively completely skipped because execution instantly violently ejects upwards looking for a rescue block.
questions.explanation: Hitting `raise` is like pulling an ejection seat. The method ends in that exact millisecond.

questions.question: Can you have multiple completely different `rescue` blocks stacked on top of each other?
options: Yes, totally! You can cleanly rescue a TypeError differently than a ZeroDivisionError in the exact same `begin`.
Yes, but they must all do the same thing.
questions.explanation: Stacking specific rescues lets your app perfectly precisely dictate the defensive response based exactly on the damage type.

questions.question: Is it completely legal to manually type `raise ArgumentError.new("Oops")` intentionally?
options: Yes! It's exactly how defensive programming aggressively works.
No, only Ruby is legally allowed to spawn core Error objects.
questions.explanation: You are actually highly encouraged to throw core Ruby errors beautifully yourself if you encounter awful data.

questions.question: What is the most beautifully ordered structure for maximum defensive safety?
questions.explanation: You start (`begin`), aggressively catch any bombs mid-air (`rescue`), and then finally meticulously wipe the floors when it's all over (`ensure`).
